parents.mother_name: Wife of David Daggett (first name pending research)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Justice's daughter married Pope Dwight's son
narrative_blocks.body: Her father David Daggett served as Chief Justice of Connecticut and US Senator from Connecticut; her husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight was a son of Pope of Yale Timothy Dwight IV. The 1811 marriage joined two of New Haven's leading Federalist families in one household.

parents.mother_name: Wife of David Daggett (first name pending research)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Justice's daughter married Pope Dwight's son
narrative_blocks.body: Her father David Daggett served as Chief Justice of Connecticut and US Senator from Connecticut; her husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight was a son of Pope of Yale Timothy Dwight IV. The 1811 marriage joined two of New Haven's leading Federalist families in one household.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, a U.S. senator and Yale Law founder

narrative_blocks.detail: Sereno Edwards Dwight served as Chaplain of the Senate of the United States from December 16, 1816 to December 19, 1817. He was ordained at Park Street Church, Boston on September 3, 1817, and served as its pastor 1817-1826 — where he greatly influenced the young hymn writer Ray Palmer, author of "My Faith Looks Up to Thee." As 3rd President of Hamilton College 1833-1835, his career was cut short by accidental mercury poisoning that interfered with his work both in Boston and at Hamilton College, making "his life after 1839 solitary and comparatively uninfluential." He produced the definitive 10-volume Life and Works of Jonathan Edwards (1830) — he was Edwards's great-grandson — which remains a key scholarly edition.
narrative_blocks.body: Served as Chaplain of the United States Senate 1816-1817; ordained at Park Street Church, Boston; served as 3rd President of Hamilton College 1833-1835. His career was cut short by accidental mercury poisoning.

After Yale 1803 + Yale tutor + New Haven lawyer he was licensed to preach in 1816 and chosen Chaplain of the United States Senate that December. He served one Congressional year until December 1817, then was ordained pastor of the Park Street Church Boston where he served nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Third president of Hamilton College, then mercury poisoned
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the third President of Hamilton College in Clinton NY from 1833 to 1835 — his brother Benjamin Woolsey Dwight was the college's treasurer at the same time. His career was wrecked shortly after by accidental mercury poisoning; the last decade of his life was 'solitary and comparatively uninfluential.'

narrative_blocks.header: Ten-volume Jonathan Edwards edition still a primary source
narrative_blocks.body: As a great-grandson of Jonathan Edwards, he published the Life and Works of Jonathan Edwards in ten volumes in 1830 — still consulted as a key primary source on the Northampton theologian. He also wrote the Life of David Brainerd (1822) and The Hebrew Wife (1836).

narrative_blocks.header: First sermon at Center Church, father's last
narrative_blocks.body: Licensed to preach October 1816, he delivered his first sermon at Center Church New Haven; that same afternoon in the same pulpit his father Pope Dwight preached what proved to be his last sermon. Six weeks later he was elected Chaplain of the U.S. Senate; the next year Lyman Beecher preached his ordination at Park Street Church Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Only child died on the day of birth
narrative_blocks.body: In August 1811 he married Susan Edwards Daggett, second daughter of CT Chief Justice David Daggett. Their only child died on the day of its birth; Susan herself, weakened by chronic dyspepsia, died in 1839 at her father David Daggett's New Haven house in her 52nd year, leaving Sereno childless and increasingly solitary for eleven more years.

artworks.blurb: Portrait by John Vanderlyn at Hamilton College President's House — given to college 1984 by Col. Dwight Garrison USAR.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: Chief Justice of the Connecticut Supreme Court 1832-1834

career.notes: Associate Judge 1826-1832; Chief Justice 1832-1834. Decided the famous Crandall v. State case 1834 (free Black education).
Kent Professor of Law at Yale, one of the founders of Yale Law as an institution.

cross_connections.display_label: the Dwight minister who married his daughter Susan

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Justice of Connecticut, ally of Pope Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: He served as US Senator from Connecticut 1813-1819 and as Chief Justice of the Connecticut Supreme Court 1832-1834. With James Hillhouse and Pope of Yale Timothy Dwight IV he was an indomitable foe of Jeffersonian republicanism in Connecticut — one of the trio of Federalist leaders who kept the state in the Federalist column for decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Nineteen children, only three lived to adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: He and his wife had nineteen children together but only three survived to adulthood. One of those three was Susan Edwards Daggett (1788-1839), who married Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight (Pope of Yale's son) in 1811 — bringing the Daggett and Dwight families together through what later became a name partner law firm in New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1783, Kent Professor at Yale Law
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1783, admitted to the CT bar 1786. From 1824 until his death he was Kent Professor of Law at Yale Law School — one of the early figures who built the law school into a national institution. Mayor of New Haven 1828-1830.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.
